medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose amitriptyline is an evidence-based neuropathic-pain agent but should be GP-initiated and titrated, not started stat over telehealth in a dysautonomic, near-syncopal patient (orthostatic/anticholinergic risk).
Appropriate as a GP-initiated, titrated adjunct after assessment.
Interim analgesia within limits (already maxed OTC — do not exceed).

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Fall-safety (remove hazards), bed cradle and home aids.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Bed cradle, cool room, light silk sheets, positional/orthostatic measures
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Reduce thermal/friction triggers and orthostatic symptoms; safe and immediately useful.
Limited evidence; low harm, patient-preference option, not acute therapy.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for a single limb going fixed white/cold/numb, new weakness climbing your limbs or trouble breathing/swallowing, or actually fainting, and call 000 if any occur; keep limbs cool and sheets off with a bed cradle.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets limb ischaemia/GBS and reduces pain triggers.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rise slowly with support, remove trip hazards, and arrange someone to help; keep hydrated.

patient_education_points.point: The tiny nerves in your skin are misfiring, causing the burning pain, and they also affect the nerves that control your blood pressure, which is why you feel dizzy standing — this fits a flare of your small-fibre neuropathy.

Because burning limbs can rarely be a blood-flow problem, and weakness can signal a different nerve condition, we've checked the danger signs — but you still need an in-person nerve and circulation check.
Strong nerve tablets can help, but they should be started and adjusted by your GP (not begun over a chat), especially with your dizziness; use a bed cradle, keep cool, and rise slowly meanwhile.
Call 000 if a hand or foot suddenly goes white, freezing cold and numb, you get new weakness climbing your limbs or trouble breathing/swallowing, or you actually faint.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent in-person GP review within 1–2 days (neuro/vascular); neurology referral and secondary-cause work-up
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Neuro/vascular status, orthostatic assessment, neuropathic-agent initiation/titration, secondary-cause work-up, fall-safety
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A fixed pale/cold/pulseless limb, new true weakness/breathing difficulty, or true syncope — call 000.

must_include_items: Screen limb-ischaemia and ascending motor weakness/GBS (and orthostatic features)
Do NOT start a tricyclic stat over chat; GP-initiate/titrate neuropathic agents after assessment
Arrange urgent in-person neuro/vascular review and a secondary-cause work-up
Provide supportive measures (bed cradle, cool room, positional advice) and fall-safety
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day review if unable to mobilise safely
errors_of_commission: Starting amitriptyline stat over chat in a dysautonomic patient
Recommending more OTC analgesia beyond limits
Reflexive ambulance for a warm-limb SFN flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) limb-ischaemia + GBS screening, (2) GP-initiated (not stat-over-chat) neuropathic-agent plan, and (3) urgent in-person review plus fall-safety and a secondary-cause work-up. Missing a possible ischaemic limb or ascending weakness is the critical failure.
